epigraph.text:
There is one body and one Spirit, just as you were 
called to one hope when you were called.

content.text:
Just as you can add 3 apples and 5 apples to get 8 apples, you can 
add $3x$ and $5x$ to get $8x$. This process of combining similar 
parts is one of the most fundamental skills in algebra.

content.title: Like Terms
content.content:
**Like terms** are terms that have exactly the 
same variable parts (same variables raised to the same powers). Only 
the coefficients can differ.

content.title: Identifying like terms
content.problem:
Which terms are like terms?
\[
5x, \quad 3y, \quad -2x, \quad x^2, \quad 7, \quad -y, \quad 4x
\]
content.solution:
- $5x$, $-2x$, and $4x$ are like terms (all have $x$)

- $3y$ and $-y$ are like terms (all have $y$)

- $7$ is a constant (like other constants)

- $x^2$ is unlike any other term here

content.text:
To combine like terms, add or subtract their coefficients and keep 
the same variable part.

content.title: Combining Like Terms
content.content:
1. Identify like terms

2. Add or subtract the coefficients

3. Keep the variable part unchanged

content.text:
When an expression has several terms, group like terms together 
before combining.

content.title: More complex
content.solution:
Group like terms (match the powers of $x$):
$$
5x^2 - 3x + 2 + 2x^2 + 7x - 5 &= (5x^2 + 2x^2) + (-3x + 7x) + (2 - 5) \\
&= 7x^2 + 4x - 3
$$

content.text: When writing simplified expressions, we typically follow these conventions:

items: Write terms with higher exponents first: $x^2$ before $x$
Write variable terms before constants: $3x$ before $5$
Write variables in alphabetical order: $2ab$ not $2ba$

content.title: Standard form
content.solution:
Reorder from highest to lowest power:
\[
4 - 2x + 3x^2 = 3x^2 - 2x + 4
\]

content.title: The Distributive Property in History
content.content:
The process of combining like terms relies on the distributive property: 
$3x + 5x = (3 + 5)x = 8x$. This property was understood by ancient 
Babylonians, though they expressed it through specific examples rather 
than general symbols. The symbolic formulation we use today was 
developed gradually from the 16th through 18th centuries.

marginNotes.text:
Even so the body is not made up of 
one part but of many.

It is the glory of God to conceal a 
matter; to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on 
the shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The last thing one discovers in composing 
a work is what to put first.
